description:
You are an IT-support technician working on ticket #4231.  
    The ticket asks you to bring a tiny set of configuration files that were staged on a “remote share” into a local work directory so that another team can inspect them.

    Starting conditions you can rely on:
      • The “remote” data has already been mounted for you at  
        /home/user/remote_share/configs  
        (treat it exactly as if it were on a remote server; do NOT alter its contents).  
      • Your current working directory is /home/user.  
      • You do NOT need root or sudo.

    Your assignment:

    1. Create a new local workspace directory called  
       /home/user/support-sync/configs  
       making sure the full parent path exists.

    2. Synchronise every file that is currently present in  
       /home/user/remote_share/configs  
       into the newly-created /home/user/support-sync/configs directory.  
       • Use rsync in archive mode so that timestamps and permissions are preserved.  
       • There are only regular files, no sub-directories.  
       • Make sure that, after the task, the two directories have byte-for-byte identical files.

    3. Produce an audit log file named /home/user/support-sync/sync.log  
       EXACTLY in the following format (this is what the automated test will parse):  
         • One line per file that was synchronised.  
         • The line must contain only the base name of the file (no path), in lowercase, with no leading/trailing spaces.  
         • Lines must be sorted in ASCII alphabetical order.  
         • The file must end with a single trailing newline character.  
       Example format (do NOT hard-code these names):  
         filename_a.conf  
         filename_b.ini  
         filename_c.txt⏎  ← (that newline at the end is mandatory)

    4. When you are done, display to the console a single line summary in the form  
          Synced N files  
       where N is the number of files you transferred. This console output is only for the human reviewer; the automated test will inspect the files on disk.

    The automated test will verify:
      • Directory /home/user/support-sync/configs exists.  
      • It contains the exact same files, with identical content, as /home/user/remote_share/configs.  
      • /home/user/support-sync/sync.log exists and matches the precise format rules above.  
      • The list of names inside sync.log corresponds exactly to the actual files that were copied.

    Perform whatever terminal commands you consider necessary to achieve the above.  
    Remember: do not modify the source directory in /home/user/remote_share in any way.
truth:
Initial remote files that are already present before the agent starts:
      Path: /home/user/remote_share/configs/app.conf
      Contents: "ENV=prod\n"

      Path: /home/user/remote_share/configs/settings.ini
      Contents: "version=2\n"

      Path: /home/user/remote_share/configs/readme.txt
      Contents: "This is the remote config directory.\n"

    Final state that the grading script will expect:

    1. Directory /home/user/support-sync/configs must exist.

    2. Exact copies of the three files (byte-identical to the originals) must be located at:
        /home/user/support-sync/configs/app.conf
        /home/user/support-sync/configs/settings.ini
        /home/user/support-sync/configs/readme.txt

    3. File /home/user/support-sync/sync.log must exist with the following byte-exact content:
        app.conf\n
        readme.txt\n
        settings.ini\n

       (That is three lines, each terminated with “\n”, and nothing else.)

    4. The agent should have printed to the terminal:
        Synced 3 files

    Permissions: all files and directories in /home/user/remote_share are read-only for the agent; files created under /home/user/support-sync should inherit default user writable permissions.
name: task_000000_ea6efedb
